title: A Historical Index of Data
subtitle: From given facts to databases, personal information, and training material.
notes: The two panels use different time scales so the contemporary period can be read rather than compressed.
The 1630-1800 span is a pre-corpus dictionary-attestation zone; Ngram frequency begins at 1800.
Phase and branch bands are visual structure, not hard historical boundaries.
Relationship arcs are curated semantic-functional links, not strict etymological descent or causal proof.
Google Books Ngram values are printed-book visibility signals, not full social usage.

panels.label: Long Formation
panels.summary: Data develops from lexical attestation and given facts into statistical, administrative, machine-processable, and early database-era systems.

phases.label: Given / Observed
phases.summary: Data as given facts, observations, measurements, and evidence.

phases.label: Statistical / Administrative
phases.summary: Data becomes institutional: statistics, records, surveys, and organized administrative material.

phases.label: Processing / Computing
phases.summary: Data becomes machine-processable information: input, output, storage, and processing.

phases.label: Database / Network
phases.summary: Structured storage, classification systems, personal information, and early networked data practices emerge.

functions.label: analysis
functions.meaning: material for interpretation, modelling, and insight

functions.label: governance
functions.meaning: protected, regulated, risky, or rights-bearing object

frequency.quality: dictionary-attestation anchor before Google Books Ngram coverage; frequency trace begins in 1800
dictionary-attestation anchor before Google Books Ngram coverage; frequency trace begins in 1800
not in generated data_frequency.json; carried from foundation scan summary

frequencySeries.source: Google Books Ngram, English corpus, case-insensitive aggregate, smoothing 0

panels.label: Contemporary Expansion
panels.summary: The recent scale expands data into platform traces, governance objects, commercial analytics, decision logic, and AI training material.
panels.hinge: Around 2005, data moves from database-era infrastructure into web-scale, platform, governance, and AI contexts.

phases.label: Platform / Analytics
phases.summary: Data expands through platforms, user traces, and large-scale analytics.

phases.label: Governance / AI
phases.summary: Data becomes a governed object, decision resource, and training material.

functions.label: platform
functions.meaning: user traces, logs, interfaces, platform exhaust

functions.label: decision
functions.meaning: data as organizational or automated action logic

functions.label: training
functions.meaning: model input, learning material, synthetic examples

requires_manual_review; Ngram signal remains below threshold, display year is curated for AI-era semantics

frequencySeries.source: Google Books Ngram, English corpus, case-insensitive aggregate, smoothing 0

caveat: Frequency and visibility are based on printed-language and curated lexical evidence. The index shows historical relations, not strict causality.

contemporaryEvidence.purpose: Supplement Chart 1 with contemporary evidence sources for 2020-2026 terms; this layer supports provenance and confidence rather than changing the visual index by itself.

sourceAudits.name: EU AI Act, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, EUR-Lex
sourceAudits.method: Official EUR-Lex text was verified as an authority source; local Node fetch returns 202/empty, so this layer records clause-level anchors instead of automated phrase counts.

verifiedAnchors.data protection: present: recitals and provisions refer to EU data protection law and personal data safeguards
verifiedAnchors.data governance: present: Article 10 requires data governance and management practices for high-risk AI training/validation/testing data sets
verifiedAnchors.training data: present: Article 10 covers training, validation and testing data sets for high-risk AI systems
verifiedAnchors.synthetic data: present: Article 10 refers to synthetic or anonymised data for bias detection/correction safeguards
verifiedAnchors.data privacy: related: privacy-preserving measures and personal data protection appear, but not as a stable exact phrase

sourceAudits.caveat: Legal-text mentions indicate normative salience, not general usage frequency.

sourceAudits.name: Stanford HAI AI Index 2025 web report pages
sourceAudits.method: Fetched public Stanford HAI report web pages and counted case-insensitive exact phrase mentions across accessible HTML.
sourceAudits.caveat: This is a web-page count, not full-PDF extraction. It records AI Index thematic salience rather than corpus frequency.

sourceAudits.name: arXiv API, CS/AI/ML subset
sourceAudits.method: Attempted arXiv API totalResults queries for exact phrases in cs.AI, cs.LG, cs.CL, and stat.ML; the API returned 503/429 during this run, so term counts are deferred.
sourceAudits.caveat: arXiv counts are academic metadata visibility, not broad social usage; phrase search can miss variants and synonyms.

sourceAudits.name: Wikimedia Pageviews API, English Wikipedia
sourceAudits.method: Fetched monthly en.wikipedia all-access user pageviews for matching concept pages from 2016 through 2025.
sourceAudits.caveat: Pageviews approximate public attention to concept pages, not phrase frequency or semantic emergence.

sourceAudits.name: NOW Corpus, English-Corpora.org
sourceAudits.method: Not fetched: no stable public API in this project environment; recommended manual export for 2010-present term counts.
sourceAudits.caveat: Best fit for contemporary news/web visibility, but should be imported from an authorized corpus export.

sourceAudits.name: Google Trends
sourceAudits.method: Not fetched: Google does not provide a stable official public API; unofficial endpoints would be fragile.
sourceAudits.caveat: Search interest is useful after the Ngram cutoff but is not comparable to corpus frequency.

sourceAudits.name: COCA, English-Corpora.org
sourceAudits.method: Not fetched: no stable public API in this project environment; recommended manual export by register/genre.
sourceAudits.caveat: Strong for genre/register comparison through 2019, weaker for 2020-2026 gaps.

sourceAudits.name: Oxford English Dictionary
sourceAudits.method: Not fetched: subscription source; use for manual attestation-year verification.
sourceAudits.caveat: Best source for earliest attestation, but quotation/citation reuse requires a separate editorial workflow.

caveats: Counts are not directly comparable across sources because legal text, report pages, academic metadata, and pageviews measure different kinds of visibility.
NOW, COCA, and OED remain manual-review sources unless authorized exports are provided.
Google Trends is recorded as a candidate post-Ngram proxy but was not fetched because there is no stable official API.
